segment-text: Written by Josh Singer & Tom McCarthy
INT. POLICE STATION, BOSTON - NIGHT, 1976
A quiet, cold winter night. An OLDER COP emerges from an INTERVIEW ROOM, walks down a long hall.
Boston, MA - December, 1976
He reaches the front desk. A YOUNG COP looks to him, curious, as he lights a cigarette.
How's that going? OLDER COP
The mother's bawling and the uncle's pissed off.
She's not married?
Divorced with four kids. I guess
Father was helping out.
Helping out?
The Older Cop shrugs. The front door opens and BURKE, FRESH FACED, 32, walks in. He wears a dark overcoat. OLDER COP Hey, Mr. Burke. They're in the back talking to the Bishop.
And Father?
The Older Cop nods in the other direction.
We put him in the break room. Any press?
Some guy from the Citizen, but we sent him away. None of the big papers.
Let's keep it that way.
Burke walks toward the interview room.
(off Burke) Who's that? OLDER COP Assistant DA.
Gonna be hard to keep the papers away from the arraignment. OLDER COP What arraignment?
He's matter of fact. The young cop reacts, looks towards the PRIEST (GEOGHAN) sitting in the break room.
INT. POLICE STATION, INTERVIEW ROOM - MOMENTS LATER
A BISHOP, 50s, talks in hushed tones with SHEILA, 33. BOYS, 7 and 9, sit coloring.
Sheila, you know what good work the church does in the community. I give you my personal guarantee that I'm going to take Father out of the parish and this will never happen again.
Burke enters, eyes Sheila nervously fingering ROSARY BEADS.
We'll just be another moment, Paul.
Of course, Father.
The Bishop turns back to Sheila. Burke eyes the kids.
Now Sheila, I'm going to give you my personal card...
EXT. POLICE STATION - LATER

The Young Cop stands outside smoking. He watches as the Bishop and Father Geoghan exit the police station and get into a TOWN CAR, a DRIVER behind the wheel. As they drive away, we HOLD ON the cop. PROCESSING.
INT. BOSTON GLOBE, NEWSROOM - DAY, 2001
A large newsroom. REPORTERS and EDITORS gathered.
Boston, MA - July, 2001
DEPUTY MANAGING EDITOR BEN BRADLEE JR., 50s, gruff, stands in front with an older reporter, STEWART, 60s. And a cake.
Losing good reporters, it's tough. And losing one of our best... well to put it as eloquently as I can, it's a real kick in the ass.
A few polite chuckles from those in the crowd.
But Robby's known Stewart longer than most of you have been alive so I'm gonna turn it over to him. Robby?
Ben looks to WALTER 'ROBBY' ROBINSON, 55, Boston Everyman.

Thanks, Ben. I think. And although I take issue with the reference to my seniority, I will say Stewart's departure is especially painful for me. You see, for... what? 20 years now, Stewart's been more than willing to part with his money at the poker table... (Laughter) And I got a kid in college. More laughter.
Yeah, but I'm gonna keep playing.
Problem solved. Godspeed, buddy. Everyone's laughing now.
Robby connects in a warm, easy way.

Now, Stewart, I find the timing of your departure a bit disconcerting. The corner office sits empty, the new editor arrives on Monday, so forgive me, buddy, but I gotta ask... what the hell do you know?
The room hoots and laughs. Stewart covers his mouth. Speak no evil. More laughter takes us to...
INT. GLOBE, HALL/STAIRWELL - LATER
SACHA PFEIFFER, 28, wholesome, no bullshit, and MATT CARROLL, mid 40s, mustache, family man, walk down the hall, into a stairwell and down a set of stairs, both carrying cake.
INT. GLOBE, HALL OUTSIDE SPOTLIGHT - MOMENTS LATER
They emerge on the floor below, Sacha eating her cake.
How's the cake?
It's really good. Saving yours?
Nah, it's for Mike. I can never eat at those things. They kind of depress me.
I know. (taking a bite) You see the numbers from Washington? Yeah.
Very interesting.
I think so too.
They walk into -8
INT. GLOBE, SPOTLIGHT OFFICE - CONTINUOUS
CRAMPED OFFICE. Small windows. MIKE REZENDES, late 30s, good looks, so-so haircut, sits at his UNHOLY MESS of a desk.

Uh huh. Uh huh. Uh huh. Dan, I talked to my guy at Justice, I know you guys have been dropping cases... (then) Look, Dan, I'm not asking if the PD's lying, I know they are. All I'm asking is who's behind it?
Matt sets the cake by Mike, sits at a desk full of FAMILY PHOTOS. Sacha checks messages at a tidy desk, full of books.

I get it. You don't want to talk. (listens) I'm not mad, Dan, I'm hungry. We've been talking so long, I missed lunch. So I'm going to go eat, which gives you an hour to decide if you want to be on the right side of this or read about it in the paper like everyone else.
Mike hangs up the phone, digs into the cake.
You think Cahill's got something?
Maybe, I just don't think this story's for us.
Ben likes it.
Yeah, it's not bad, it's just not Spotlight.
Robby enters.
What's just not Spotlight?
The PD numbers.
The numbers story. thin.
You got Cahill to talk?
(busted) No. But I will.
It feels
Then we'll know if it's thin.
Robby goes into his office, bit of an edge to him.
How'd it go upstairs? Okay.
Robby gave a good speech.
I heard Lubin and Connor are going to the Times.
Come on, really?
Yeah, I heard the same thing.
They're leeching us.
At least they're sending us a guy from Miami to run things. That should help.
Has Baron even spent time in Boston?
I don't think he's ever been here before. Maybe we should buy him a map.
I have a friend down at the Herald, said Baron cut 15% of the staff. Great.
Mike rolls his chair back, calls out to Robby in his office.
Hey Robby, the new boss coming in to
I have no idea.
Aren't you sitting down with him?
Robby looks up, fucking Rezendes, always snooping.
You think it's about Spotlight?
I don't know, Mike, but I do know Ben needs an answer on the crime numbers story.
I'm telling you, I really don't think that story's for us.
So you did get Cahill to talk then?
Mike, annoyed, wheels back to his desk, picks up his phone...
INT. FOUR SEASONS HOTEL, RESTAURANT - LATER
Marty sits at a table reading "The Curse of the Bambino," a NOTEPAD beside him.
Marty looks up and rises to greet Robby.
Walter Robinson. Robby.
Thanks for taking time out of your weekend, Robby.
My pleasure. (they sit) What are you reading?
Marty holds up a book.
'The Curse of the Bambino.'
(sitting) That's a great book. The Globe has season tickets at Fenway, once you settle in.
Thank you. Uh, to be honest, I'm not much of a baseball fan. I'm just trying to get a feel for city. Are you originally from Boston?
I am, born and raised. A lot of the newsroom is from here. It really feels like a local paper in that way.
And did that change at all after the paper was sold to the Times?
No. I don't think that had a big impact.
Good. (then) So, you're an editor for, uh, the Spotlight team?
I prefer to think of myself as more of a player-coach. But yes. Are you familiar with Spotlight? No.
Not particularly.
Well, we're a four person investigative team. We report to Ben Bradlee Jr. and we keep our work confidential.
What are you working on now?
We just put out a piece on this shoddy construction outfit and right now we're trolling around for our next story.
How long does that typically take?
Hard to say. A couple of months.
A couple of months.
Yeah, we don't like to rush it. Once we settle on a project, we can spend a year or more investigating it.
Marty jots down some notes. Robby sees he's writing a lot.
Is that a concern?
Not necessarily. Uh, but from what I understand readership is down, the Internet is cutting into the Classified business and, uh, I think, uh, I'm going to have to take a hard look at things.
So you anticipate more cuts?
I would assume so, yes, but what I'm more focused on right now is finding a way to make this paper essential to its readers.
I'd like to think it already is.
Fair enough. I just think we can do better.
EXT. BOSTON GLOBE - DAY
The front of the Globe. door. He walks inside. Marty gets out of a cab, shuts the
INT. GLOBE, NEWSROOM - DAY
Ben strides across the newsroom.
Mr. Bradlee.
Robby falls in.
Hey. Saw the email on the PD numbers. Looks interesting. Yeah.
There's something there.
How much longer you need?
Another week.
(realizing) Where are you going?
To the 10:30. You?
Since when?
Technically, I am an editor.
Technically. Your sit-down with Baron go that well?
I couldn't get a read on him.
That's a first.
How 'bout you?
(shrugs) He's a barrel of laughs.
INT. GLOBE, LARGE CONFERENCE ROOM - LATER
Editors around a horseshoe table, side seats filled. It's QUIET, nervous eyes on Marty, who reads some notes. Ben and editor HELEN DONOVAN, 50s, to either side. Robby sits off to the side, WATCHING.
Morning everyone. Let's get started. Do you want to say something, Marty?
Uh, sure. (to the group) Hello. As most of you know by now, my name is Marty Baron, I'm delighted to be here. If you can tell me your name as we go around, that would be helpful.
Marty looks back to his notes.
Peter?
Ben nods to METRO EDITOR PETER CANELLOS, 40s.
Peter Canellos, Metro. We've got a major Big Dig closure that's just been scheduled for early August... Marty makes notes.
Off Ben and Robby, trading a look.
INT. GLOBE, NEWSROOM - LATER
Mike, cuppa coffee, walks over to STEVE KURKJIAN's desk. 60s. Hey Steve.
Crappy game last night.
They can't hit worth a nickle.
Mike looks toward the conference room of editors.
What's Eileen McNamara doing in the 10:30?
Do you need something, Mike? No.
Just curious.
I got work to do. Go be curious somewhere else, will you?
INT. GLOBE, LARGE CONFERENCE ROOM - LATER
The meeting is wrapping up.
...and it looks like Pedro's gonna be out until September 1st. Jimmy says he'll be back this year, but the doc didn't seem so sure.
When's the Pats opener? Laughter.
Except Marty.
Is that everyone?
Yes, that's it.
Great, thank you. Uh, did everyone read Eileen McNamara's column this weekend?
The room reacts. Huh? Editors look at EILEEN MCNAMARA, 50s.
That's the Geoghan case?
Yes, what's the folo on that?
It's a column, what kind of folo were you thinking?
Well, apparently this priest molested kids in six different parishes over the last thirty years and the attorney for the victims, Mr...
Thanks, Eileen. Mr. Garabedian says Cardinal Law found out about it fifteen years ago and did nothing.
I think that attorney's a bit of a crank. And the Church dismissed the claim.
He said, she said.
Whether Mr. Garabedian is a crank or not, he says he has documents that prove the Cardinal knew.
As I understand it, those documents are under seal.
Silence. No one knows what to do. Robby watches.

Okay, but the fact remains, we have a Boston priest abused 80 kids, we've got a lawyer who says he has proof Law knew about it, and we've written all of... (checking his notes) ...two stories on this in the last six months? (then) This strikes me as an essential story for a local paper. At the very least, we should be going after those documents.
How would you like to do that?
Well, I don't know what the laws are here, but in Florida we would go to court.
Robby raises an eyebrow. In fact, the whole room does.
You want to sue the church?
Technically we wouldn't sue the Church. We would file a motion to lift the seal on those documents.
The church will read that as us suing them. So will everybody else.
Good to know.
INT. GLOBE, BEN'S OFFICE - LATER
Ben and Robby walk into Ben's office.
Gutsy call for the first day.
That's one word for it.
How do you think it's gonna play down front?
I think Gilman's gonna shit a brick. Knock. Knock. Mike's at the door. Hey.
Door.
BEN
Mike shuts the door, looks at Robby.
How'd it go?
Baron wants to sue for the sealed docs in the Geoghan case.
He wants to sue the church? great.
We covered Geoghan when the story broke three years ago. Baron was still in Miami.
(needling Ben) I think he was at the Times then.
He wasn't here.
You think the suit has a chance?
Depends on the judge. BEN And what parish he belongs to.
Ben's phone RINGS. Ben picks it up.
Bradlee. Yeah. Okay. (hanging up, to Robby) Baron wants to talk to us. Us?
Off Robby, surprised -18
INT. GLOBE, MARTY'S OFFICE - MOMENTS LATER
Ben and Robby enter. Marty's on the phone.
Okay, thank you. Yes. Okay, thanks.
Marty hangs up as they sit.
I set up a meeting for tomorrow with outside counsel...
Yeah.
Who's the Judge on this case?
Uh, Constance Sweeney. Tough.
Why's that?
Good Catholic girl.
Marty pushes on.
Judging from what I've read, it doesn't seem like we've done a thorough investigation of the Geoghan case. Is that right?
No it's not. We looked hard at Geoghan. David Armstrong for Metro and Michael Paulson for Religion.
Marty isn't impressed.
Okay, but, uh, just so I understand, beyond our daily coverage, we haven't committed any long term investigative resources to the question of whether or not Cardinal Law knew about this?
No, we haven't.
(to Robby) And that's the kind of thing your team does?
Spotlight? Well, yeah, but we're still prospecting the Boston PD story I told you about.
Could you set it aside? We could.
Marty, in the past, Spotlight has had success in large part because they pick their own projects.
Would you consider picking this one?
INT. GLOBE, SPOTLIGHT OFFICE - LATER
Mike, Sacha, Matt all working. Sacha on the phone.
I don't know if he's writing about you but I will let him know you called. Okay. Bye.
Sacha hangs up as Robby enters.
Hey, how'd it go?
Okay. Baron wants us to scrub the Geoghan case.
That's great.
Didn't we cover Geoghan?
There's a lawyer alleging Cardinal Law knew about it.
Mitch Garabedian, right? him at the courthouse.
Can you get to him?
I only interviewed him once. kind of a character.
I like characters.
He's I'll take him.
He's yours. Also, we should talk to the lawyer who repped the victims in the Porter case. Eric MacLeish.
That guy was always on TV.
The Porter case? Remind me?
Father Porter, similar story, molested dozens of kids in Fall River about ten years ago.
So we're dropping the Boston PD?
I vote yes.

No, we're just setting it aside for now. I don't need to tell you guys, we need to be more discreet than usual. Everybody's gonna be interested in this, not just the Herald. I don't want the Chancery getting wind of this before we know what we have.
Good luck with that.
They reach for phones and computers, Robby leaves them to it.
EXT. SACHA'S GRANDMOTHER'S HOUSE, SOUTHIE - NIGHT
The quiet street dips down, disappearing into the harbour. Sacha and Hansi get out of their car, cross the street.
INT. SACHA'S GRANDMOTHER'S HOUSE, DINING ROOM - NIGHT A cozy dining room. Sacha, her GRANDMOTHER and her husband
HANSI finish dinner. Sacha's grandmother wears a gold cross.
When are you leaving, Nana?
The bus is picking us up in the
church parking lot at nine o'clock.
Is Father Dominic going?
No. He said, he's very unlucky. But luck has nothing to do with it.
Hansi can't help but smile.
Nana won a hundred and sixty dollars last time.
INT. SACHA'S GRANDMOTHER'S HOUSE, KITCHEN - NIGHT
Hansi and Sacha wash dishes. They talk quietly.
You gonna tell her?
We don't even know if there's a story yet.
"Globe sues church?" be a story.
There's gonna Sacha continues rinsing, glances at the small, porcelain Virgin Mary on the window sill above the sink.
INT. GLOBE, LARGE PUBLISHER'S FOYER - DAY

Marty ascends a large, open air staircase in the upscale business side of the building. He steps off, heads down the hall then stops, looks around. Lost in his own building. A YOUNG EMPLOYEE walks past.
Excuse me, do you know where the Publisher's office is?
The employee points. Marty heads that direction.
INT. GLOBE, PUBLISHER'S OFFICE - DAY
RICHARD GILMAN, 50s, Brooks Brothers suit, finishes a call. Marty is sitting on a couch in large, plush office opposite.
Thanks, Tom. Will do. (hangs up, to Marty)
How are you, Marty? Settling in?
Yes, I think so.
Good. What can I do for you?
I'd like to challenge the protective order in the Geoghan case.
You want to sue the Catholic Church?
We're just filing a motion. But yes.
You think it's that important? Yes.
I do.
Because, obviously, the Church will fight us very hard on this. Which won't go unnoticed by our subscriber base. 53% of them are Catholic.
Uh, I think they'll be interested.
Gilman considers for a long moment, looks unsettled.
Marty nods, gets up to go.
Marty? Lake Street will probably contact you about a face to face with the Cardinal. It's customary.
They already have. It's set up for next week.
I wouldn't mention this.
INT. GLOBE - DAY
Matt walks down a row of cubicles, disappears down a spiral staircase.
INT. BOSTON GLOBE LIBRARY - CONTINUOUS
Matt descends the staircase and arrives at the front desk.
Hey Lisa. Could you pull all the relevant clips on that for me?
He hands her a sheet of paper.
She looks at it.
This is for Spotlight?
Reacts.
Just drop them off when they're ready, thanks.
Matt walks off.
INT. MITCHELL GARABEDIAN'S OFFICE - DAY
Mike steps off an elevator and into a DUMP of an office. eyes a small reception desk covered with FILE BOXES. He Hello.
Hello?
Mike peeks through an open door... a small office, crammed FLOOR TO CEILING with BOXES, each with GEOGHAN written on it.
Can I help you? Mike turns. A PARALEGAL pokes her head out of a cubicle.
Oh. Hi. I'm Mike Rezendes from the Boston Globe. I'm here to see Mitchell Garabedian.
He's on a call. Please have a seat.
Mike grabs a seat, checks his watch. He hears YELLING coming from behind a closed door. Garabedian?
INT. ONE INTERNATIONAL PLACE, LOBBY - DAY
Robby and Sacha stride across an enormous, marble and stone lobby, a STARK CONTRAST to Garabedian's digs.
INT. GREENBERG TRAURIG, CONFERENCE ROOM - DAY
ERIC MACLEISH, 40s, good looks, quick with a story and a smile, walks into the posh room, finds Sacha and Robby. ERIC MACLEISH The famous Walter Robinson in my
MacLeish and Robby shake hands.
Good to see you again, Eric. Pfeiffer, Eric MacLeish.
Nice to meet you. Do you play golf?
(caught off guard) Uh, no.
Good. Your colleague took some money off me at a charity event last year.
Lucky putt. I actually had my eyes closed.
MacLeish smiles. Chummy.
So, how can I help you?
You're familiar with the Geoghan case?
Sure. Eighty plaintiffs. All individual cases, Garabedian must be swimming.
And the allegations against Cardinal Law?
MacLeish considers the question.
Look, it's tricky. You need to understand these are shitty cases. The statute of limitations is only 3 years and most of these victims don't come forward until long after that.
Why is that?

They're kids. Shame. Guilt. And they come from tough neighborhoods, nobody wants to admit this kind of thing. So you're screwed on the time limit and even if you argue your way around that, the charitable immunity statute caps damages at twenty grand.
Twenty grand for molesting a child?

That's the way the system works. The Church is tough. So your best shot is to try these cases in the press like I did on Porter. But most victims want nothing to do with being on TV. And Mitch isn't exactly a people person.
So if Garabedian can't get these victims to talk to the press...
Then he has a long road ahead of him. My guess? He doesn't have anything on Law.
You think he's bluffing?
I think he's grandstanding to cut a better deal.
Seems a bit reckless.
Have you met Mitch Garabedian?
INT. GARABEDIAN'S OFFICE - LATER

Mike, still waiting, checks his watch. He hears more YELLING behind the door. Suddenly the door opens. A SHORT MAN exits. Mike looks at the paralegal, points: "Is that him?" She shakes her head, walks off screen. Mike watches her go. Then, impatient, he stands, walks into --
INT. GARABEDIAN'S OFFICE, CONFERENCE ROOM - CONTINUOUS
A small boardroom. MITCHELL GARABEDIAN, thin, 50s, sits at a table buried in paperwork. He's abrasive, to say the least. Hi.
Who are you?
Mike Rezendes from the Boston Globe. I had an appointment with you about an hour ago.
I can't talk to you, I'm very busy. (calling to reception) Sharon?!
Sharon went out for coffee, she said she'd be right back, I'm following up on an article...
The one in the Phoenix?
No. In the Globe.
Did you see the one in the Phoenix?
No, I didn't.
I thought it was very good. I have a copy here somewhere.
He starts to dig through the heap of papers.
That's okay. I'll track it down. I'm actually following up on a column that Eileen McNamara wrote for the Globe about your suit.
Suits. There are eighty-four of them, it's not a class action. You should get your facts straight.
True to word.
Garabedian is a bit of crank.
You're right. I should. I'm just trying to get some background information on the Geoghan case...
You're not recording this are you?
No, I wouldn't do that without asking.
(brusque) I can't show you the Church documents if that's what you're after, they're under seal.
I know that.
Do you know they've tried to bring me before the Massachusetts Board of Bar Overseers three times? They're watching me very closely.
The Church?

Yes, the Church. They'd like to get me disbarred. In fact, put that away. (off Mike's pad) Put it away! I don't want you recording this in any way, shape or form. Not on paper, not on tape, nothing. In fact, I probably shouldn't even be speaking to you.
Look, Mr. Garabedian, I know there are things you can't tell me. But I also know that there's a story here. And I think it's an important story.
I already talked to the Phoenix.
Yeah, and there's a reason I didn't see it, nobody reads the Phoenix anymore. They're broke, they don't have any power. The Globe does. And if we cover this story, everybody will hear about it.
The Church thinks in centuries, Mr. Rezendes. You think your paper has the resources to take that on?
Yeah, I do. But if you don't mind me asking, do you?
Mike's question is non-threatening, but Garabedian reacts. He stands, walking Mike to the door...
I don't have the time to talk to you, Mr. Rezendes, I'm very busy. (calling out)
But before he can push Mike out the door, Mike turns back -MIKE
Call me tomorrow. I need to think about it.
INT. GLOBE, LIBRARY - DAY

A Button is pushed, CONTROL PANEL lights up. A rotating library clip file comes to stop. A few old newspaper clips are pulled. A folder from the LARGE PHOTO clip file is pulled. A Nexis search on a computer screen. A Micro Fiche search on another screen. A PRINTER spits out the Micro Fiche article. The PRINT OUT out is added to a STACK OF ARTICLES and dropped into a file marked Porter.
INT. GLOBE, HALLWAY - LATER
CLOSE ON the same file. Moving. REVEAL the folder is now one of many on a METAL CART that an intern (WANDA) wheels down the hall.
INT. GLOBE, SPOTLIGHT OFFICE - LATER
A desk covered with old newspaper clips, Matt and Sacha read. A knock... Wanda in the door. With the metal cart.
I got more clips from the library.
Leave 'em right there.
Wanda the intern unloads them beside Matt's desk.
You guys doing a story on the Church?
No. We are not doing a story on the Church.
Wanda blanches, leaves. Robby pours some coffee.
Matt, you find anything on this guy
No.
Who is he?
He's part of a victims' organization. Kurkjian ran a story on him just after the Porter case.
There's a victims' organization?
Yeah, it's called SNAP, Survivors Network of those Abused by Priests.
Crummy acronym.
You want me to track him down?
Yeah. Bring him in. How much longer do you need to get through the clips?
A few days. There's a lot and Lisa's still sending stuff up. of it is on Porter.
Hey guys... I think I got another priest. Robby and Sacha turn.
Matt stares down at a clip.
Liam Barrett. Molested some kids in Philly then was moved to Boston, did the same thing then was moved again. Really?
Sounds like Geoghan.
Is that one of our clips?
Yeah. Byline's Diego Ribadeneira, 1997. Must've been back when he was working religion.
Was there any folo?
Not much. One short piece. Looks like the Church settled the case. (then) Don't you know Jim Sullivan? Yeah, why?
Looks like the Church brought him in to help out.
Matt shows Robby the clip. Robby reads, surprised.
EXT. FENWAY PARK - NIGHT
Establishing. From up above, a game in progress.
INT. FENWAY PARK - NIGHT
Mike and Matt sit with Ben and Steve Kurkjian at the game. called third strike and the crowd groans.
Jesus. Has anybody but Manny gotten the ball out of the infield? Matt, who's been scoring the game, checks.
Nope.
I don't know why you bother with that thing.
It distracts me from the game.
Know what else is good for that?
Kurkjian holds up an empty beer cup.
Good idea. My round. Anybody want food? Hot dog.
Matt heads off. Matt stands.
Ben grabs some peanuts, turns to Mike.
You guys making any progress?
Absolutely. It's a good story.
Why? Cause you're another lapsed Catholic pissed off at the Church?
We've got some good stuff. Like what?
Mike glances over at Kurkjian.
I'm just watching the game.
We got another priest, Liam Barrett.
We reported on him. (off Mike's look) We did, I'm just saying.
Okay, but the guy was shuffled from parish to parish every few years, just like Geoghan and Porter. I think there's a pattern.
Ben looks at Kurkjian, who's not impressed.
Sounds thin. What else you got?
Sacha found this guy from a victims' organization, Phil Saviano. From SNAP? him too.
Oh boy. We reported on
I thought you were watching the game, Steve?
That guy's pretty banged up, Mike.
We ran a couple stories on him, the guy would not stop writing letters.
He wants a holy war.
So he's not worth taking to?
Where are you on Garabedian?
I'm working on it.
Robby said MacLeish thinks he's bluffing.
I think there's something there, Ben.
Get me something solid or I'm taking you off it. I don't want us chasing our tails on this. Off Mike -37
EXT. WOLLASTON GOLF CLUB, 8TH TEE, MILTON, MA - DAY
JIM SULLIVAN, 50s, lines up a tee shot. Smacks it. ROBBY and TWO OTHER GOLFERS watch as Jim talks to his ball...
Stay away from that trap.
You couldn't reach that trap in a million years, partner.
One of the other golfers (PAUL) tees up his ball.
I've reached that trap before. Yeah.
Now Paul, maybe.
The group laughs.
EXT. WOLLASTON GOLF CLUB, 8TH HOLE FAIRWAY - DAY
Robby and Jim walk down the fairway. Robby checks the card.
How we doing?
We're good. We're two up.
Good. (then) How's the new editor working out?
Well, he doesn't like baseball. he seems like a smart guy.
Yeah, I just read an article about him. Said he's the first Jewish editor at the Globe.
Must have been a slow news day.
He got a family?
No, he's not married. Divorced?
Don't think so.
So the new editor of the Boston Globe is an unmarried man of the Jewish faith who hates baseball?
Robby half smiles, shrugs.
They should have given it to Ben. He's a Bradlee for crying out loud.
He didn't raise his hand fast enough. (then) By the way, I was reading about this priest, Father Barrett. Said you were involved in the case.
Jim reacts, surprised.
Barrett, yeah. Bad egg. Lake Street asked for help, I pitched in.
The victim said Cardinal Law knew about Barrett when he came from Philly.
You know I can't talk about the case, Robby.
